skill.name: service-mesh-observability
skill.description: Guide service mesh observability work across Istio, Linkerd, Prometheus, Grafana, Jaeger, Kiali, and OpenTelemetry.
skill.summary: Set up service mesh metrics, tracing, dashboards, alerts, and SLO-focused troubleshooting.
skill.author: wshobson

security_audit.summary: The high static score is mostly false positives from Markdown code fences, PromQL windows, and documentation links. The skill is a Markdown guide with no executable package code, but several deployable examples use broad access or insecure defaults, so publication should include a security warning.

medium_findings.title: Insecure Observability Access Defaults
medium_findings.description: The Kiali example uses anonymous authentication and access to all namespaces. If applied unchanged, it can expose mesh topology and telemetry beyond the intended audience.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The YAML directly shows anonymous auth and a wildcard namespace scope. The context is a template, so this is risky guidance rather than confirmed malicious behavior.

medium_findings.title: Open Telemetry Example Uses Broad Network Bindings
medium_findings.description: The collector example binds several receivers to 0.0.0.0 and disables TLS verification for the Jaeger exporter. This can expose telemetry endpoints in permissive networks.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The endpoint and TLS settings are explicit in the sample configuration. Risk depends on cluster network policy and whether users deploy the example unchanged.

medium_findings.title: Cluster Mutating Shell Pipeline
medium_findings.description: The Linkerd example pipes generated manifests directly into kubectl apply. This is a common installation pattern, but users should review generated manifests before applying them.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command clearly changes cluster state, but it uses the standard Linkerd CLI flow. I did not find evidence of hidden command execution.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backtick Detections Are Mostly False Positives
low_findings.description: The static external command alerts mostly point to fenced Markdown examples, not executable Ruby or shell code. Only the Linkerd bash block contains user-run commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged regions are Markdown code fences containing YAML, PromQL, JSON, and bash examples. There is no runnable skill script that automatically executes these snippets.

low_findings.title: Path Traversal Detection Is A PromQL Regex False Positive
low_findings.description: The flagged path traversal sequence is the PromQL pattern response_code=~"5.." for 5xx status codes. It is not a filesystem path.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The full line is inside a Grafana query expression for HTTP response codes. No file path or filesystem operation is present.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto Detections Are Query Syntax False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography alerts match PromQL windows, 5xx patterns, quantiles, and Markdown text. I did not find MD5, SHA1, DES, or similar cryptographic use.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines are observability metric expressions and descriptive text. No cryptographic API, hash algorithm selection, or key material handling appears in the file.

low_findings.title: Documentation Links Are Not Exfiltration Endpoints
low_findings.description: The hardcoded public URLs in the resources section link to official documentation. They do not receive data from the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The links are plain Markdown references to project documentation. There is no code path that sends local data to these URLs.

dangerous_patterns.title: Anonymous Dashboard Configuration
dangerous_patterns.description: A deployable Kiali template enables anonymous access and wildcard namespace visibility. This pattern should be hardened before production use.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The risky settings are explicit and deployable. The risk is configuration exposure, not malware.

dangerous_patterns.title: Insecure TLS Exporter Setting
dangerous_patterns.description: The OpenTelemetry exporter sets tls.insecure to true. This weakens transport validation for telemetry export.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The configuration explicitly disables TLS security for the exporter. Deployment context determines practical impact.

dangerous_patterns.title: All Interface Telemetry Bindings
dangerous_patterns.description: The collector example binds telemetry endpoints to 0.0.0.0. Network policy should restrict access before use.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The endpoints are visible in the sample configuration. The exposure risk depends on cluster service configuration and network controls.

content.value_statement: Service mesh teams need clear visibility into latency, errors, traffic, and service dependencies. This skill provides practical templates for tracing, metrics, dashboards, and alerting across common mesh tools.
actual_capabilities: Explains service mesh observability concepts and golden signals.
Provides Prometheus and Grafana examples for Istio metrics.
Shows Jaeger and OpenTelemetry tracing configuration patterns.
Lists Linkerd viz commands for dashboards, routes, taps, and edges.
Provides Prometheus alert rules for error rate, latency, and certificate expiry.
Summarizes practical best practices for sampling, retention, labels, and dashboards.
limitations: It is a Markdown guide and does not deploy tools automatically.
Examples require review before use in production clusters.
Some templates use permissive defaults that should be hardened.
It does not validate compatibility with every mesh or Kubernetes version.

use_cases.title: Build Mesh Dashboards
use_cases.description: Create initial Prometheus and Grafana views for service request rate, error rate, latency, and topology.

use_cases.title: Investigate Service Latency
use_cases.description: Use metrics, traces, and Linkerd viz commands to narrow down slow routes and backend dependencies.

use_cases.title: Define Mesh SLO Alerts
use_cases.description: Draft alerting rules for high error rates, high P99 latency, and expiring mesh certificates.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Basic Mesh Monitoring
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to outline a basic service mesh observability setup for Istio. Include metrics, tracing, dashboards, and alerts.

prompt_templates.title: Create Service Health Queries
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to design Prometheus queries for request rate, 5xx error rate, P99 latency, and service topology.

prompt_templates.title: Troubleshoot A Latency Incident
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to create a troubleshooting flow for a service mesh latency issue across metrics, traces, and dependency views.

prompt_templates.title: Harden Production Observability
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to review the provided service mesh observability templates for production readiness, security, sampling, and cost controls.

output_examples.input: Set up mesh monitoring for an Istio namespace.
output_examples.output: A structured plan covering Prometheus scraping, Grafana panels, Jaeger tracing, Kiali visualization, and core SLO alerts.

output_examples.input: Debug high P99 latency between frontend and backend.
output_examples.output: A step-by-step investigation path using latency histograms, route-level metrics, distributed traces, and service dependency checks.
A concise alert plan for error rate, latency, certificate expiry, and dashboard ownership with production hardening notes.

best_practices: Review every deployment template before applying it to a cluster.
Use secure dashboard authentication and least-privilege namespace access.
Tune tracing sampling, retention, and label cardinality for production cost control.
anti_patterns: Do not expose observability dashboards with anonymous production access.
Do not bind telemetry endpoints broadly without network policy controls.
Do not apply generated manifests without reviewing cluster impact.

faq.question: Does this skill deploy service mesh observability tools?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and templates that a user can adapt and apply.

faq.question: Which service meshes does it cover?
faq.answer: It focuses on Istio and Linkerd, with examples for common observability tools.

faq.question: Can I use it with OpenTelemetry?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes an OpenTelemetry Collector example for traces and metrics.

faq.question: Is it production ready as written?
faq.answer: No. Review authentication, namespace scope, TLS settings, network access, and sampling before production use.

faq.question: Does it include alerting examples?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes alert ideas for high error rate, high latency, and certificate expiry.

faq.question: Can Claude, Codex, and Claude Code use this skill?
faq.answer: Yes. The report marks support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
